step.title: Present and Align
step.goal: Present the design proposal to the user as a brain-surgery conversation. Capture the user's response (confirm / redirect / reverse / abort) and pass it to step 4 for classification and strike-counter handling. This step is critical="true" — cannot be skipped, even in YOLO mode. Per QRDSPIV §D: "D is a conversation, not a document."

substep.title: Present the Proposal
substep.ask:
Present the user with the full Design Summary draft. Format the ask as:
Here's how I understand the work:
{{session.design_summary_draft}}
Brain-surgery question: does this look right?
Options: - Confirm (Recommended if it looks right): the design is correct; lock and move to ticket-structure - Redirect scope: scope is wrong; describe what's off - Adjust the chain: scope is right but the artifact chain is wrong; describe the correction - Adjust risk: scope and chain are right but a risk is mis-stated; describe - Reverse a previous decision: I want to revisit something previously locked (DR or Q↔R outcome); name what - Abort: stop the workflow
Capture the user's response verbatim where possible. Classify in step 4.
Store as {{session.user_response}}: {classification_hint, verbatim, details}.

substep.title: Capture Verbatim
substep.action:
Record the user's exact words in {{session.strike_log}} as a timestamped entry (whether or not this turns into a strike, the verbatim record helps step 4 classify correctly).
Entry format: { timestamp: ISO 8601, ticket_design_iteration: N, user_response_classification_hint: "confirm" | "redirect" | "adjust_chain" | "adjust_risk" | "reverse" | "abort", verbatim: <user's text>, notes: <agent's initial assessment, refined in step 4> }

substep.title: Display Response Summary
substep.output:
User response captured: - Classification hint: {{session.user_response.classification_hint}} - Length: {{verbatim_word_count}} words
Proceeding to redirect/strike handling.

section.title: Why this is a single conversational ask
section.description: D-phase is brain-surgery — one big presentation, one big response. The proposal is dense; the user needs to take it in as a whole and react. Breaking it into per-section asks ("does the artifact chain look right? does the risk look right? does the decision-points list look right?") fragments the alignment conversation and loses the "do these pieces work as a WHOLE" signal that brain-surgery alignment is meant to produce.

rules.rule: "Confirm" is always offered FIRST in the options list with "(Recommended)" when the draft looks defensible
If the draft has known weaknesses (e.g., a risk area was speculatively included), surface that in the ask text: "I'm less sure about the second risk — wanted to flag for review"
Do not present the design as a fait accompli; the user CAN redirect or reverse

section.title: The verbatim record matters
section.description: Subtle word choice in the user's response shapes step 4's classification. "I'd like to revisit Option A vs Option B" is a REVERSE (it's questioning a locked alternative selection). "I think the artifact chain should also include a UX spec update" is an ADJUST_CHAIN (additive, not a reversal). The verbatim record preserves the signal for step 4 to read.

section.title: Critical step, no YOLO skip
section.description: critical="true" on this step is per the workflow engine's critical-rules. YOLO mode auto-completes everything else, but D-phase alignment is the single point where the user gates the downstream phases — bypassing it produces ticket-structure on an unconfirmed design.
